verdicts.note: Score is airtight, but highlight 5 cites [S1] for 'the local party organization backed his opponent'; that fact is not in Salomon's S1 pamphlet — his dossier sources it to the contest file (contest S3). Highlight citation should be corrected or the clause dropped.

Upheld at +1 medium, but note the basis partly leans on the 'too moderate' framing (S5/S7), which is intra-party positioning rather than a spending stance; the load-bearing evidence is the fully-fund-public-education plank (S2). Do not escalate this score.

Upheld, but the highlight and basis omit the dossier's moderating nuance that she opposes data-center subsidies 'though not the facilities themselves' and supports AI/quantum development paired with regulation [S5][S6]. Since pole_b is defined by subsidy skepticism and guardrails, +2 still fits, but the display text slightly overstates her hostility to the industry.

Direction is defensible but wholly indirect: the dossier contains no tax position — no opposition to any tax, no cut proposal. The score is inferred from his spending/regulation framing of affordability ('rather than revenue shortfalls') [S2][S3], which per the guide is indirect inference and merits low, not medium, confidence.

'Parental involvement and transparency' [S1][S2] is softer than pole_a's 'parental control of curricula,' and the dossier records no position on reproductive rights, gender content, or curriculum fights. The basis leans on church-ministry/homeschool biography, which is identity inference, not a stated position. Direction plausible, but this is indirect inference: low confidence.

missing.basis: Sponsored HB 1052, updating the hate-crime statute so bias need only be a partial motive (passed the Senate, 2025) — matches pole_b's anti-discrimination-law element. A single narrow bill with no campaign statements on reproductive/LGBTQ+ issues, so low confidence only.
